common.tagline: Smart safety for pets — starting in Dublin.

home.metaTitle: TALOA — Smart Safety for Pets in Dublin
home.metaDescription: TALOA gives your pet a smart safety tag. If they're ever lost, whoever finds them can reach you in seconds. Starting in Dublin.
home.title: TALOA
home.subtitle: Smart safety for pets — starting in Dublin.
home.clubSubtitle: We're building our Dublin partner network. Club members get exclusive discounts and perks — new partners added monthly.
home.clubPerk1Desc: Exclusive deals with Dublin pet services — grooming, walking, training and more.
home.clubPerk2Desc: Earn points every time you protect your pet. Redeem for free services and quarterly prize draws.
home.clubPerk3Desc: First access to new features, new partners and TALOA events in Dublin.

card.noTag: This pet doesn't have a tag yet, so there's no card to share.
card.shareHint: Anyone with this link can view the card — great for a pet sitter, a new vet, or a hotel.
card.notFoundBody: This card link is invalid or the tag isn't active.

care.shareIntro: Create a temporary link with {name}'s full profile to share with a pet sitter, hotel, or new vet. You can revoke it anytime.
care.disclaimer: TALOA is not a vet. In an emergency, contact a vet immediately.
care.expiredBody: This care link is no longer active. Ask the owner for a new one.
care.shareDiaryHint: Let the carer see recent activities and upcoming vaccines — read only.

emergency.metaDescription: A directory of Dublin veterinary clinics, including 24-hour emergency care. Find a vet fast when your pet needs help.

emergency.subtitle: Dublin veterinary directory.
emergency.disclaimer: TALOA provides this directory for information only. Always call the clinic to confirm availability and suitability for your pet's species. This is not veterinary advice.

ai.disclaimer: TALOA AI is not a vet. For emergencies, call a vet immediately.
ai.greeting2: Ask me about pet safety, lost & found, or finding a vet in Dublin.
ai.reuniteGreeting: Hi! It looks like you found {petName}. I’m TALOA AI — I’m here to help you reunite them with their owner safely. Is {petName} with you right now?

careGuide.title: Care Guide
careGuide.pdfError: Could not generate the PDF. Try again.
careGuide.loadError: Could not load the care guide. Try again.
careGuide.upgradeTitle: Care Guide is a Plus feature
careGuide.upgradeBody: Upgrade to Plus for species-specific care guides you can download as a PDF.
careGuide.disclaimer: General care guidance, not veterinary advice. Always consult an exotics vet.

tips.reptile_habitat_secure: Use a secure, escape-proof enclosure with a locking lid — reptiles are skilled escape artists.
tips.reptile_habitat_size: Size the enclosure for your reptile's adult size, not the size it is today.
tips.reptile_habitat_hides: Provide at least two hides: one on the warm side and one on the cool side.
tips.reptile_habitat_substrate: Choose a substrate that is safe for your species — avoid loose substrates that can cause impaction in young reptiles.
tips.reptile_feeding_research: Research your species' exact diet — insectivore, herbivore, carnivore or omnivore — from reliable sources.
tips.reptile_feeding_supplements: Dust feeder insects with calcium and vitamin supplements as recommended by an exotics vet.
tips.reptile_feeding_water: Keep fresh water available at all times, even for desert species.
tips.reptile_feeding_live_prey: Never leave uneaten live prey in the enclosure — it can injure your reptile.
tips.reptile_env_temp_gradient: Create a temperature gradient with a warm basking side and a cooler side so your reptile can self-regulate.
tips.reptile_env_thermostat: Always control heat sources with a thermostat and check temperatures with a reliable thermometer.
tips.reptile_env_uvb: Provide UVB lighting appropriate for your species and replace bulbs on schedule — output fades before the light dies.
tips.reptile_env_humidity: Monitor humidity with a hygrometer and keep it in your species' ideal range.
tips.reptile_env_no_direct_sun: Never place a closed enclosure in direct sunlight — it can overheat fatally in minutes.
tips.reptile_handling_support: Support the whole body when handling — never grab a reptile by the tail.
tips.reptile_handling_hygiene: Wash your hands before and after handling — reptiles can carry salmonella.
tips.reptile_handling_short: Keep handling sessions short and calm, especially with new or young animals.
tips.reptile_handling_avoid_after_feeding: Avoid handling right after feeding or during shedding.
tips.reptile_hygiene_spot_clean: Spot-clean droppings daily and do a full substrate change on a regular schedule.
tips.reptile_hygiene_disinfect: Disinfect the enclosure and decor with reptile-safe products only.
tips.reptile_hygiene_hands: Good hand hygiene protects both you and your reptile.
tips.reptile_hygiene_quarantine: Quarantine any new reptile away from existing animals before introducing them.
tips.reptile_enrichment_climbing: Add climbing branches, rocks and textures suited to your species.
tips.reptile_enrichment_rearrange: Occasionally rearrange decor to encourage exploration.
tips.reptile_enrichment_feeding_variety: Vary how you offer food — tongs, scatter or puzzle feeding — to encourage natural behaviour.
tips.reptile_enrichment_digging: Offer a digging area if burrowing is natural for your species.
tips.reptile_warning_appetite: Watch for prolonged loss of appetite or weight loss — note it down and mention it to your vet.
tips.reptile_warning_shed: Stuck shed, especially around eyes and toes, needs attention — ask an exotics vet for guidance.
tips.reptile_warning_lethargy: Unusual lethargy, swelling, discharge or abnormal droppings are reasons to book an exotics vet visit.
tips.reptile_warning_find_vet: Find a reptile-experienced exotics vet BEFORE you need one — they are rarer than dog and cat vets.
tips.bird_habitat_cage_size: Choose the largest cage you can — wide enough to stretch both wings — with safe bar spacing for your species.
tips.bird_habitat_perches: Offer natural wood perches in varied diameters to keep feet healthy.
tips.bird_habitat_location: Place the cage in a social room but away from the kitchen, drafts and direct sun.
tips.bird_habitat_liner: Use a cage grate or paper liner you can change easily every day.
tips.bird_feeding_pellets: Feed a quality pellet base plus fresh vegetables daily — seeds alone are not a complete diet.
tips.bird_feeding_toxic_foods: Never feed avocado, chocolate, caffeine or alcohol — they are toxic to birds.
tips.bird_feeding_water: Refresh drinking water at least twice a day.
tips.bird_feeding_seed_limit: Keep seeds and treats to a small share of the diet to avoid obesity.
tips.bird_env_temperature: Keep the room temperature stable and away from drafts and radiators.
tips.bird_env_light: Provide natural daylight or full-spectrum lighting during the day.
tips.bird_env_sleep: Give your bird 10-12 hours of dark, quiet sleep — cover the cage if needed.
tips.bird_env_fumes: Never use non-stick cookware (PTFE), aerosols or scented candles near birds — the fumes can be fatal.
tips.bird_handling_trust: Build trust gradually with short, positive training sessions — never force interaction.
tips.bird_handling_support: Hold gently supporting the body — never squeeze a bird's chest.
tips.bird_handling_supervise: Supervise all out-of-cage time in a bird-proofed room.
tips.bird_handling_room_hazards: Before free flight, check windows, mirrors, ceiling fans, open water and other pets.
tips.bird_hygiene_cage: Clean perches, bowls and the cage floor regularly.
tips.bird_hygiene_bathing: Offer regular baths or gentle misting — most birds love it.
tips.bird_hygiene_nails_beak: Have nails and beak checked by an avian or exotics vet rather than trimming at home.
tips.bird_hygiene_hands: Wash your hands before and after handling your bird.
tips.bird_enrichment_foraging: Use foraging toys so your bird works for food like it would in the wild.
tips.bird_enrichment_rotate: Rotate toys weekly to keep the cage interesting.
tips.bird_enrichment_social: Spend social time daily — birds are flock animals and loneliness causes stress.
tips.bird_enrichment_shredding: Provide safe paper or soft wood items to shred.
tips.bird_warning_fluffed: A bird sitting fluffed up at the bottom of the cage needs a vet — do not wait.
tips.bird_warning_droppings: Persistent changes in droppings are worth a call to an avian or exotics vet.
tips.bird_warning_breathing: Tail bobbing while breathing or breathing effort is an emergency — contact a vet immediately.
tips.bird_warning_hiding_illness: Birds instinctively hide illness — by the time they look sick they need a vet urgently.
tips.rabbit_habitat_space: Give enough space to take at least three full hops in a row and stand upright on hind legs.
tips.rabbit_habitat_hideout: Provide a hideout where your rabbit can retreat and feel safe.
tips.rabbit_habitat_proofing: Rabbit-proof cables, skirting boards and toxic houseplants in any free-roam area.
tips.rabbit_habitat_flooring: Use soft, non-slip flooring — wire floors hurt rabbits' feet.
tips.rabbit_feeding_hay: Unlimited fresh hay should be about 85% of the diet — it keeps teeth and gut healthy.
tips.rabbit_feeding_greens: Offer a variety of fresh leafy greens daily.
tips.rabbit_feeding_pellets: Measure pellets — a small daily portion, not free-fed.
tips.rabbit_feeding_gradual_changes: Change any food gradually over 1-2 weeks — sudden changes upset the gut.
tips.rabbit_env_heat: Keep your rabbit cool — temperatures above 25°C risk heatstroke; provide shade and cool tiles.
tips.rabbit_env_ventilation: House in a dry, well-ventilated space out of drafts.
tips.rabbit_env_predators: Keep hutches and runs secure from foxes, cats and birds of prey — even the sight of predators causes stress.
tips.rabbit_env_routine: Rabbits are creatures of habit — keep feeding and cleaning routines consistent.
tips.rabbit_handling_hindquarters: Always support the hindquarters when lifting — rabbits can injure their spine kicking.
tips.rabbit_handling_never_ears: Never pick a rabbit up by the ears or scruff.
tips.rabbit_handling_ground: Most rabbits prefer interaction at ground level over being carried.
tips.rabbit_handling_stress: Learn stress signs — thumping, flattened ears, hiding — and give space when you see them.
tips.rabbit_hygiene_litter: Clean the litter tray daily — rabbits can be litter trained like cats.
tips.rabbit_hygiene_flystrike: Check the rear end daily in warm months — flystrike is a life-threatening emergency.
tips.rabbit_hygiene_nails: Trim nails regularly or have a vet or groomer do it.
tips.rabbit_hygiene_brushing: Brush during moulting seasons — swallowed fur can block the gut.
tips.rabbit_enrichment_tunnels: Offer tunnels and cardboard boxes to run through and hide in.
tips.rabbit_enrichment_chew: Provide untreated wood, willow balls and safe chew toys.
tips.rabbit_enrichment_companion: Rabbits are social — a bonded, neutered companion is the best enrichment of all.
tips.rabbit_enrichment_forage: Scatter-feed greens and hay to encourage natural foraging.
tips.rabbit_warning_stasis: A rabbit that hasn't eaten or produced droppings for 12 hours is an emergency — call a vet immediately.
tips.rabbit_warning_dirty_rear: A dirty rear end needs same-day attention, especially in summer.
tips.rabbit_warning_head_tilt: A sudden head tilt or loss of balance means an urgent vet visit.
tips.rabbit_warning_teeth: Drooling, dropping food or a wet chin can mean tooth problems — have a rabbit-savvy vet check.
tips.small_mammal_habitat_cage: Choose a cage with the right size and bar spacing for your species, with a solid floor.
tips.small_mammal_habitat_bedding: Use paper-based or aspen bedding — avoid cedar and pine shavings, which harm airways.
tips.small_mammal_habitat_hideouts: Provide hideouts and nesting material for security and warmth.
tips.small_mammal_habitat_social: Research whether your species lives alone or in groups — hamsters are solitary, guinea pigs need company.
tips.small_mammal_feeding_base_diet: Feed a species-specific base diet — needs differ a lot between hamsters, guinea pigs, gerbils and rats.
tips.small_mammal_feeding_hay_vitc: Guinea pigs need unlimited hay and daily vitamin C — they cannot make their own.
tips.small_mammal_feeding_water: Check the water bottle works and refill it daily.
tips.small_mammal_feeding_treats: Keep sugary fruits and shop treats occasional — obesity is common.
tips.small_mammal_env_sun_drafts: Keep the cage out of direct sun and drafts.
tips.small_mammal_env_temperature: Maintain a stable room temperature — sudden cold can trigger torpor in hamsters.
tips.small_mammal_env_quiet: Give nocturnal species a quiet spot during the day to sleep.
tips.small_mammal_env_ventilation: Ensure good ventilation — closed tanks trap ammonia from urine.
tips.small_mammal_handling_scoop: Scoop up with both hands, low over a soft surface in case of jumps.
tips.small_mammal_handling_taming: Tame gradually with treats and short sessions — let the animal come to you.
tips.small_mammal_handling_children: Always supervise children handling small pets.
tips.small_mammal_handling_escapes: Handle in a closed, escape-proofed room — small mammals are fast.
tips.small_mammal_hygiene_bedding: Spot-clean soiled bedding often and do a full change weekly.
tips.small_mammal_hygiene_bottles: Wash bottles and bowls regularly to stop bacteria building up.
tips.small_mammal_hygiene_teeth_nails: Check teeth and nails — overgrowth is common and needs a vet.
tips.small_mammal_hygiene_hands: Wash hands before and after cleaning or handling.
tips.small_mammal_enrichment_wheel: Provide a solid-surface exercise wheel large enough that the back stays straight.
tips.small_mammal_enrichment_chew: Offer safe chew items — rodent teeth grow continuously.
tips.small_mammal_enrichment_tunnels: Add tunnels and multi-level areas for exploring.
tips.small_mammal_enrichment_scatter: Scatter food in bedding to encourage natural foraging.
tips.small_mammal_warning_lethargy: Lethargy or not eating in a small mammal always deserves a prompt vet call.
tips.small_mammal_warning_digestion: Diarrhoea or a wet tail in hamsters is an emergency — see a vet the same day.
tips.small_mammal_warning_breathing: Clicking or noisy breathing needs an exotics vet check.
tips.small_mammal_warning_fast_decline: Small bodies decline fast — when in doubt, call an exotics vet early rather than waiting.
tips.fish_habitat_tank_size: Choose a tank sized for your fish's adult size and group needs — most 'starter' tanks are too small.
tips.fish_habitat_cycling: Cycle the tank for several weeks before adding fish so beneficial bacteria can establish.
tips.fish_habitat_filtration: Use a filter rated for your tank size with flow your species can handle.
tips.fish_habitat_tank_mates: Research compatibility before mixing species — mismatches cause stress and injuries.
tips.fish_feeding_small_amounts: Feed small amounts once or twice a day — only what is eaten in a couple of minutes.
tips.fish_feeding_remove_uneaten: Remove uneaten food so it doesn't rot and pollute the water.
tips.fish_feeding_variety: Vary the diet with foods suited to your species — flakes, pellets, frozen or live as appropriate.
tips.fish_feeding_no_overfeed: Overfeeding is the most common cause of poor water quality — when in doubt, feed less.
tips.fish_env_water_testing: Test water regularly for ammonia, nitrite, nitrate and pH — poor water quality is the top cause of illness.
tips.fish_env_water_changes: Do partial water changes weekly rather than rare full changes.
tips.fish_env_dechlorinate: Always treat tap water with a water conditioner before it touches the tank.
tips.fish_env_temperature: Keep temperature stable with a heater and thermometer for tropical species.
tips.fish_handling_minimize: Handle and net fish as little as possible — handling stress weakens their immune system.
tips.fish_handling_soft_net: When netting is needed, use a soft, fine-mesh net and move slowly.
tips.fish_handling_acclimate: Acclimate new fish slowly to the tank's water before releasing them.
tips.fish_handling_tap_water: Never let untreated tap water touch your fish or filter media.
tips.fish_hygiene_filter_media: Rinse filter media in removed tank water, never under the tap — it kills the good bacteria.
tips.fish_hygiene_algae: Keep algae in check with regular maintenance rather than harsh chemicals.
tips.fish_hygiene_substrate: Vacuum the substrate during water changes to remove waste.
tips.fish_hygiene_quarantine: Quarantine new fish and plants before adding them to the main tank.
tips.fish_enrichment_plants: Provide plants and hiding places — cover makes fish feel safe and show natural behaviour.
tips.fish_enrichment_mates: Keep shoaling species in proper groups — a lone shoaling fish is a stressed fish.
tips.fish_enrichment_decor: Vary the environment with decor suited to your species.
tips.fish_enrichment_current: Match water flow to your species — some love current, others need calm water.
tips.fish_warning_gasping: Fish gasping at the surface usually means a water problem — test the water immediately.
tips.fish_warning_fins_spots: Clamped fins, white spots or fuzzy patches are signs to act on quickly.
tips.fish_warning_appetite: A fish that stops eating or swims erratically needs attention.
tips.fish_warning_test_and_vet: When something looks wrong: test the water first, then consult an aquatic or exotics vet.
tips.other_habitat_research: Research your species' natural habitat from reliable sources and replicate it as closely as you can.
tips.other_habitat_secure: Use a secure, escape-proof enclosure — exotic pets are often skilled escapers.
tips.other_habitat_size: Size the enclosure for the animal's adult size and activity level.
tips.other_habitat_substrate: Choose bedding or substrate known to be safe for your species.
tips.other_feeding_research: Learn your species' exact dietary needs from reliable, species-specific sources.
tips.other_feeding_water: Provide fresh water at all times in a form your species can use.
tips.other_feeding_human_food: Avoid human food unless it is confirmed safe for your species.
tips.other_feeding_schedule: Keep a consistent feeding schedule and watch that food is actually being eaten.
tips.other_env_research: Find out your species' ideal temperature, humidity and light cycle.
tips.other_env_measure: Measure conditions with a thermometer and hygrometer instead of guessing.
tips.other_env_sun_drafts: Keep the enclosure out of direct sun and cold drafts.
tips.other_env_stability: Stable conditions matter more than perfect ones — avoid sudden changes.
tips.other_handling_research: Learn the safe handling technique for your specific species before picking it up.
tips.other_handling_hands: Wash your hands before and after handling.
tips.other_handling_short: Keep handling sessions short and calm until trust is built.
tips.other_handling_stress: Learn your species' stress signals and stop when you see them.
tips.other_hygiene_routine: Set a regular cleaning routine: spot-clean often, deep-clean on schedule.
tips.other_hygiene_safe_products: Use only cleaning products confirmed safe for your species.
tips.other_hygiene_quarantine: Quarantine new animals before introducing them to existing pets.
tips.other_hygiene_hands: Good hand hygiene protects you and your pet from shared infections.
tips.other_enrichment_research: Research what enrichment suits your species — climbing, digging, foraging or hiding.
tips.other_enrichment_rotate: Rotate enrichment items to keep the environment interesting.
tips.other_enrichment_natural_behaviour: Respect natural rhythms — don't wake nocturnal species for daytime play.
tips.other_enrichment_food: Use food-based enrichment to encourage natural foraging behaviour.
tips.other_warning_changes: Watch for changes in appetite, droppings, energy or appearance — with exotics, subtle changes matter.
tips.other_warning_find_vet: Find an exotics vet BEFORE you need one — not every clinic treats exotic species.
tips.other_warning_when_unsure: When unsure, call an exotics vet — early advice is cheaper and safer than waiting.
tips.other_warning_no_self_treatment: Never medicate an exotic pet yourself — treatments safe for dogs or cats can be fatal to other species.
